On September 9, 2025, Park Jin-young was appointed as the co-chairman (minister level) of the Popular Culture Exchange Committee directly under the president, Lee Jae Myung.( On March 10, 2026, Park announced he will be stepping down from JYP Entertainment's company board and focusing on his role as the co-chair of Popular Culture Exchange Committee, planning to focus on creative work as an artist, mentoring younger artists, and participating in other external activities related to the K-pop industry.(

### Acting

[edit]

In early 2011, JYP made his acting debut in _Dream High_, for which he received a nomination for New Actor of the Year at the Baeksang Arts Awards. In January 2012 he was seen in the sequel to Dream High called _Dream High 2_.( source?_] [...] 47.   ^Kim, Ju-yeon (March 10, 2026). "K-pop mogul Park Jin-young stepping down from JYP's board to focus on 'creative work'". _Korea JoongAng Daily_. Retrieved March 11, 2026.
48.   ^"Park Jin Young Cast for Sequel of "Dream High"". Soompi. November 21, 2011. Archived from the original on November 23, 2011. Retrieved November 22, 2011.
49.   ^Ho, Stewart (23 May 2012). "Official Movie Poster for Park Jin Young's Debut Movie Unveiled". _enewsWorld_. CJ E&M. Archived from the original on 5 March 2016. Retrieved 29 May 2012.
50.   ^"5 Million Dollar Man Releases Official Trailer Showing JYP Being Ridiculed". Soompi. June 25, 2012. Archived from the original on June 28, 2012. Retrieved June 25, 2012. [...] Park Jin-young (Korean:박진영; born December 13, 1971),( also known by his stage namesJ. Y. Park and The Asiansoul or the initials JYP, is a South Korean singer-songwriter, record producer, record executive, and reality television show judge.( Park rose to stardom as a singer following the release of his 1994 debut album, _Blue City_.( In 1997, he became the founder of JYP Entertainment, one of the most profitable entertainment agencies in South Korea.(

As the head of JYP Entertainment from 1997 to 2011, Park has developed and managed successful K-pop artists including 2PM, Day6, Got7, Itzy, KickFlip, Miss A, Nmixx, Rain "Rain (entertainer)"), Stray Kids, Twice, Xdinary Heroes and Wonder Girls, as well as Mandopop group Boy Story and J-pop groups Nexz and NiziU.(

**Park Jin-young** ([Korean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"):박진영; born December 13, 1971),[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-birth_date-1)[[A]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-dob-2) also known by his stage names**J. Y. Park** and **The Asiansoul** or the initials **JYP**, is a South Korean singer-songwriter, record producer, record executive, and reality television show judge.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-4)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-5) Park rose to stardom as a singer following the release of his 1994 debut album, _Blue City_.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-6) In 1997, he became the founder of [JYP Entertainment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JYP_Entertainment "JYP Entertainment"), one of the most profitable entertainment agencies in South Korea.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-7)

As the head of JYP Entertainment from 1997 to 2011, Park has developed and managed successful [K-pop](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/K-pop "K-pop") artists including [2PM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2PM "2PM"), [Day6](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Day6 "Day6"), [Got7](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Got7 "Got7"), [Itzy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Itzy "Itzy"), [KickFlip](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KickFlip "KickFlip"), [Miss A](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miss_A "Miss A"), [Nmixx](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nmixx "Nmixx"), [Rain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rain_(entertainer) "Rain (entertainer)"), [Stray Kids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stray_Kids "Stray Kids"), [Twice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twice "Twice"), [Xdinary Heroes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xdinary_Heroes "Xdinary Heroes") and [Wonder Girls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wonder_Girls "Wonder Girls"), as well as [Mandopop](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mandopop "Mandopop") group [Boy Story](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boy_Story "Boy Story") and [J-pop](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J-pop "J-pop") groups [Nexz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nexz "Nexz") and [NiziU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NiziU "NiziU").[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-8)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-9)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-10)

## Early life and education

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J.Y._Park&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Park was born in [Gwangjin District](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gwangjin_District "Gwangjin District"), [Seoul](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), South Korea.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-JYP-3) His father was a news correspondent based in the U.S. At age 9, Park moved with his mother to New York following his father's reassignment. They lived there for three years before Park returned to Seoul for high school. He later attended [Yonsei University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yonsei_University "Yonsei University"), during which time he released his first two albums. He graduated with a bachelor's in [geology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geology "Geology") in 1996.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-11) He has an older sister.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-:1-12) Park learned music composition and production for two years from [Kim Hyung-suk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kim_Hyeong-seok "Kim Hyeong-seok"), who is considered one of the best pop music composers and producers of all time in South Korea.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-13)

## Career

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J.Y._Park&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Park originally debuted as the trio boy group "Park Jin Young and the NG (New Generation)" (박진영과 신세대), with [Kim Soo-chul](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kim_Soo-chul "Kim Soo-chul") as a producer, and members Cho Hye-sung and Yoon Tae-jin. Their first album 'Floating time' (떠도는 시간) was not successful.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-14)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-15)[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-16)[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-17) Park's notability within the Korean music industry began in 1994 when he debuted as a solo artist with the song "Don't Leave Me" (날 떠나지마) from his debut album _Blue City_.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-18) During this period, he met composer and long-time collaborator [Bang Si-hyuk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bang_Si-hyuk "Bang Si-hyuk"). In 1997, Park founded his label and agency [JYP Entertainment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JYP_Entertainment "JYP Entertainment"), then known as Tae-Hong Planning Corp (대홍기획). That same year, he was tasked by EBM (now [SidusHQ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SidusHQ "SidusHQ")) with preparing the members of its project group for debut; the five-member act was eventually called [g.o.d](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G.o.d "G.o.d") and debuted two years later.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-MBC2014-19) The success of g.o.d as one of the country's most popular and bestselling groups of the early 2000s would further establish Bang and Park's reputation as hit makers.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-20)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-21)[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-22)

In 2004, Park ventured into the American music industry, becoming the first Asian producer to cross over to the U.S., producing music for [Will Smith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Will_Smith "Will Smith"), [Mase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mase "Mase") and [Cassie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cassie_(singer) "Cassie (singer)").[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-auto-23)[_[unreliable source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable\_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")_][[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-24)

In May 2008, Park collaborated with [Jackie Chan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan") to form the I Love Asia Project, prompted by the [earthquake tragedy in China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_Sichuan_earthquake "2008 Sichuan earthquake"). Park produced the song "Smile Again" along with Chan and Korean director [Kang Je-gyu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kang_Je-gyu "Kang Je-gyu") to raise funds for relief efforts in [Sichuan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan").[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-25) The song featured an array of Korean talents including actors and singers as well as [figure skater](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating")[Kim Yuna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kim_Yuna "Kim Yuna").[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-26) The music video was released on JYP Entertainment's YouTube channel on June 30.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-27)

In October 2009, Park became the first Korean songwriter, together with [RAINSTONE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rainstone "Rainstone"), to reach the [Billboard Hot 100 Chart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") with the [Wonder Girls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wonder_Girls "Wonder Girls") hit "[Nobody](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nobody_(Wonder_Girls_song) "Nobody (Wonder Girls song)")" which debuted at No. 76.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-28)

On December 3, 2009, Park released his single "No Love No More".[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-29) On April 22, 2011, Park collaborated with [Brown Eyed Girls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brown_Eyed_Girls "Brown Eyed Girls")' [Ga-In](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gain_(singer) "Gain (singer)"), releasing the duet track "Someone Else". The song charted at No. 2 on the [_Gaon_ Digital Chart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaon_Digital_Chart "Gaon Digital Chart") and sold over a million copies. This was his first release in about two years. Park followed up his success with "Someone Else" by releasing the single "You're the One" on April 28, 2012. The song rose to No. 3 and sold over 1.5 million copies.

In 2015, Park released the song "Who's Your Mama?" featuring [Lucky J](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucky_J "Lucky J")'s [Jessi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jessi_(musician) "Jessi (musician)"). The song became a hit, displacing fellow label-mates [Miss A](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miss_A "Miss A") from first spot on the Korean charts.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-30) Later that year, he participated in the [_Infinite Challenge Yeongdong Expressway Music Festival_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infinite_Challenge#Infinite_Challenge_Music_Festival "Infinite Challenge"), forming the duo Dancing Genome with comedian [Yoo Jae-suk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yoo_Jae-suk "Yoo Jae-suk") and releasing the song "I'm So Sexy".[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-auto1-31)

In 2016, Park released a single titled 'Still Alive'.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-auto1-31) During the same period, he appeared in the [Conan O'Brien](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conan_O%27Brien "Conan O'Brien") special '_Conan in Korea_', recording a song with Conan O'Brien, [Steven Yeun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steven_Yeun "Steven Yeun")&[Park Ji-min](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Park_Ji-min_(singer,_born_1997) "Park Ji-min (singer, born 1997)") titled 'Fire', with label-mates [Wonder Girls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wonder_Girls "Wonder Girls") and [Twice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twice "Twice") appearing in the music video.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-32) The song was released on Conan O'Brien's YouTube channel on April 9.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-33) Park also appeared in the Korean variety show _[Sister's Slam Dunk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sister%27s\_Slam\_Dunk "Sister's Slam Dunk")_ in 2016, producing the cast's single titled '[Shut Up](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shut_Up_(Unnies_song) "Shut Up (Unnies song)")'.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-34) The song unexpectedly achieved a real-time chart all-kill upon release.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-35)[_[unreliable source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable\_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")_] On September 22, 2016, it was reported that Park would be producing the title track of girl-group [I.O.I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I.O.I "I.O.I") for their final album prior to disbandment.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-36) The song, titled "[Very Very Very](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Very_Very_Very "Very Very Very")" was released on October 16, 2016.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_] It achieved commercial success upon release, earning a perfect all-kill on the Korean charts and topping the [_Gaon_ Digital Chart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaon_Digital_Chart "Gaon Digital Chart").[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-37)[_[unreliable source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable\_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")_][[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-38) In 2019, Park released a song titled 'Fever' featured by Superbee and [BIBI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bibi_(singer) "Bibi (singer)").

On August 11, 2020, Park released his autobiography, _Live for What?_[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-JY_Park_Story-39) One day later, Park released a new single, "[When We Disco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/When_We_Disco "When We Disco")", which was a duet with [Sunmi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunmi "Sunmi"). This was his first collaboration with Sunmi since her departure from JYP Entertainment and the [Wonder Girls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wonder_Girls "Wonder Girls").[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-40) The single peaked at #3 at the [Gaon Digital Chart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaon_Digital_Chart "Gaon Digital Chart"), becoming his sixth Top 10 song in the chart.[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-41)

On April 26, 2021, it was reported that Park and [Psy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psy "Psy"), the founder of [P Nation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P_Nation "P Nation"), will collaborate to form a new boy group each in _[Loud](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loud\_(South\_Korean\_TV\_series) "Loud (South Korean TV series)")_, which premiered on June 5, 2021, on [SBS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seoul_Broadcasting_System "Seoul Broadcasting System").[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-42)

In November 2022, Park released the single "Groove Back", which was released on November 21, but the music video was released three days prior, according to the agency.[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-43) later Park confirmed to hold a solo concert, In Japan for the first time in 7 years at Yokohama [Pia Arena MM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pia_Arena_MM "Pia Arena MM"), January 28–29, 2023.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-44) On September 16, 2024, a television special _The KBS Grand Project — 30th Anniversary Special Entertainer JYP_ aired on [KBS2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KBS2 "KBS2"). Celebrating the 30th anniversary of Park's debut, it featured appearances from g.o.d, Rain, Sunye, Sunmi, 2PM and [Twice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twice "Twice").[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-45)

Park Jin-young is celebrating his 30th debut anniversary with a solo concert, Still JYP. The concert, scheduled for December 27 to 29 at Olympic Hall in Seoul, features his greatest hits and is designed to appeal to fans of all ages. Additional tickets are available for purchase through Interpark, YES24, and Naver Reservations. Park Jin-young's legacy continues to thrive, with international performances planned.[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-46)

On September 9, 2025, Park Jin-young was appointed as the co-chairman (minister level) of the Popular Culture Exchange Committee directly under the president, [Lee Jae Myung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lee_Jae_Myung "Lee Jae Myung").[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-47) On March 10, 2026, Park announced he will be stepping down from JYP Entertainment's company board and focusing on his role as the co-chair of Popular Culture Exchange Committee, planning to focus on creative work as an artist, mentoring younger artists, and participating in other external activities related to the K-pop industry.[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-48)

### Acting

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J.Y._Park&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Acting")]

In early 2011, JYP made his acting debut in _[Dream High](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dream\_High "Dream High")_, for which he received a nomination for New Actor of the Year at the [Baeksang Arts Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baeksang_Arts_Awards "Baeksang Arts Awards"). In January 2012 he was seen in the sequel to Dream High called _[Dream High 2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dream\_High\_2 "Dream High 2")_.[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-49)[_[unreliable source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable\_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")_]

In 2011, Park made his film debut as Choi Young In, a man on a mission to hand-deliver 5 million dollars, in _Five Million Dollar Man_ with [Jo Sung-ha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jo_Sung-ha "Jo Sung-ha") and [Min Hyo-rin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Min_Hyo-rin "Min Hyo-rin").[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-50) The film was released on July 19, 2012.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-51)[_[unreliable source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable\_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")_]

## Personal life

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J.Y._Park&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life")]

### Relationships

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J.Y._Park&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Relationships")]

In 1999, Park Jin-young married Seo Yoon-jeong. In March 2009, the couple announced their divorce.[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-52) On October 10, 2013, he married a woman nine years younger than him.[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-JYP_tried_to_sell_agency-53) On January 25, 2019, his daughter was born, and Park wrote "_This Small Hand_" for her and his father, with a documentary-style music video released on February 9.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-:1-12) In the music video, it was revealed that his father was in the late stage of [Alzheimer's disease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease") and could no longer recognize him. All proceeds from the song went to the Green Umbrella Foundation ([ChildFund](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ChildFund "ChildFund") Korea) to help children in need.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-54)

### Religion

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J.Y._Park&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Religion")]

Park is a born-again Christian and leads weekly Bible practices.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-JY_Park_Story-39) Park turned to several faiths to fill the "void in [his] heart" after his divorce before finding his faith in Christianity.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-JY_Park_Story-39)

### Plagiarism allegation

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J.Y._Park&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Plagiarism allegation")]

On February 10, 2011, songwriter Kim Sin-il filed a lawsuit against Park claiming that "Someday", sung by [IU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IU_(entertainer) "IU (entertainer)") and composed by Park, plagiarized Kim's song "To My Man". Kim alleged that the beginnings of the two songs, including the jazz chords, are almost identical and sued Park for 110 million won.[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-55) Park denied the allegations, and no settlement was reached after several court hearings.[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-56) On February 10, 2012, the Seoul Central District Court ruled that "Someday" was plagiarized from Kim's song, and ordered Park to pay 21.67 million won in damages to Kim.[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-57) Park appealed the ruling, but on January 24, 2013, the Seoul High Court ruled against Park and ordered him to pay 56.93 million won to Kim.[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-58) In 2015, the Supreme Court overturned the previous rulings in light of new evidence demonstrating that the melody and chord progressions were very common, specifically citing [Kirk Franklin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kirk_Franklin "Kirk Franklin")'s 2002 song "[Hosanna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Rebirth_of_Kirk_Franklin "The Rebirth of Kirk Franklin")".[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-59)

## Philanthropy

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J.Y._Park&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Philanthropy")]

In December 2022, Park donated 500 million won each to Samsung Seoul Hospital and World Vision, an international relief and development non-governmental organization.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-60) In December 2023, he donated 200 million won each to 5 hospitals in South Korea to support the treatment of underprivileged children.[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.Y._Park#cite_note-61)

From CEO to Cultural Architect: J.Y. Park’s shift to ministerial-level leadership.

# J. Y. Park Steps Down From JYP Board, Seeks to Widen K-Culture

## As JYP's footprint expands worldwide, Park focuses on developing a better K-culture policy while bolstering creative development.

BY[Diya Mukherjee](/author/diya-mukherjee)

11 MAR 2026, 06:29 PM

[![Google Recommendation](https://img-2.outlookindia.com/outlook-business/images/google-preferred-badge.png)](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=outlookrespawn "Google Recommendation")

* J. Y. Park will stand down from JYP Entertainment's board of directors during the shareholder meeting on March 26.
* He was previously appointed as co-chair of the Presidential Committee on Popular Culture Exchange, where he will be responsible for creative leadership and policy.
* Despite stepping down, Park will remain JYP's top stakeholder, with a 15.82% stake and significant influence over the company.

J.Y. Park, an important contributor of modern K-pop, will step down from the board of JYP Entertainment as Chief Creative Officer (CCO) this month, signaling a strategic shift away from corporate governance, while moving towards global cultural policy and artistic development. As per reports and the company statement, Park will not seek reappointment as an inside director at the March 26 shareholder meeting.

By stepping down from his fiduciary position, the 54-year-old mogul is refocusing his role to emphasize his recent ministerial-level designation as co-chair of the Presidential Committee on Popular Culture Exchange. The move allows Park to step away from the rigors of daily operations while still steering the agency's newer artistic efforts and South Korea's broader "Hallyu" export strategy, including music, gaming, and film.

In the ministerial capacity as stated above, the [JYP](https://respawn.outlookindia.com/pop-culture/pop-culture-news/k-pop-agency-jyp-entertainments-revenue-more-than-doubles) founder will work beside Culture Minister Chae Hwi-young Park. As noted by Korea JoongAng Daily, JYP Entertainment said that Park plans to “focus on his creative work as an artist, mentor younger artists, and take on new external roles related to the K-pop industry.”

## **JYP Entertainment’s Legacy of Dominance**

Since establishing JYP Entertainment in 1997, Park has grown from a solo singer to a global tycoon. He guided the careers of the biggest names, ranging from the Wonder Girls and 2PM to modern icons TWICE and [Stray Kids.](https://respawn.outlookindia.com/pop-culture/pop-culture-news/stray-kids-concert-2026-film-makes-a-19m-global-box-office-debut)

His innovative leadership continues to produce record-breaking results. In 2024, Stray Kids' recent "dominATE" world tour became the highest-grossing K-pop tour leg in North American history, earning $76.2 million USD from just 13 gigs, as shown by Billboard Boxscore data. At the same time, J.Y. Park, along with JYP CEO Jung Wook, and JYP America head Shin Hyun-kuk, got placed on [Billboard](https://respawn.outlookindia.com/pop-culture/pop-culture-news/bts-stray-kids-katseye-hit-big-on-billboard-artist-index)’s Global Power Players list, thereby showcasing the label’s rising influence in overseas markets.

## **J.Y. Park to Still Have the Last Say on JYP’s Future**

Despite his departure from the board, Park's influence over the agency remains undeniable. By owning 15.82% along with related parties, he remains the company's largest stakeholder, thereby retaining his position of having the last say over the direction of the label.

The move represents a profound progression for one of the "Big Four" agencies: a transition from traditional firm management to a dual-pronged strategy in which Park molds JYP's literal sound while also guiding the worldwide policy of the [K-pop industry](https://respawn.outlookindia.com/pop-culture/pop-culture-news/k-pop-event-celebrates-philippines-korea-cultural-ties) itself.

**FY25 Q1 Earnings Note**
2025-05-15

In 2025 Q1, revenue was 140.8 BN KRW, increased by 3.1% YoY, and operating profit was 19.6 BN KRW, decreased by 41.6% YoY. Net Profit increased by 120.2% YoY to 69.3 BN KRW from the sales of DearU shares.

In Q1, music and concert sales both decreased YoY from an absence of major artists’ albums and concerts. Physical sales decreased by 3.8% YoY to 29.7 BN KRW, with new releases from early-stage artists including NMIXX mini, KickFlip debut, ITZY Yeji Solo, Xdinary Heroes mini, and NiziU single. Streaming decreased by 5.5% YoY to 15.0 BN KRW as global and domestic streaming sales dropped slightly.

Moreover, concert revenue decreased by 26.3% YoY to 21.7 BN KRW due to lack of major artists’ large-scale concerts, while there was a high base effect from Japan. In 2024 Q1, 29 Japan concerts from 2023 were reflected, but only 18 Japan concerts, including MISAMO 2 times, NiziU 12 times, and DAY6 4 times, took place in 2025 Q1, resulting in 52.7% decrease in Japan concert sales.

In Q1, global fan meetings and fan concerts were actively held to strengthen the fan base, reaching 8.8 BN KRW in appearance revenue with an increase of 86.1% YoY. NMIXX held arena-scale fan concerts in Japan and continued fan concerts in Latin America. Stray Kids also held 3 arena-scale fan meetings in Korea. Through fan meetings and fan concerts, artists solidified their global fandom by communicating more closely with their fans.

Advertisement revenue increased by 14.6% YoY, reaching 9.0 BN KRW with the expansion of artist recognition.

In Q1, MD sales recorded 32.7 BN KRW, increased by 37.1% YoY, due to Japanese MD and Stray Kids' domestic fan meeting MD sales. Japan MD sales increased by 32.8% YoY due to strong sales of MISAMO and NiziU's Japan concert MD and Stray Kids' online MD. In the case of Stray Kids' domestic fan meetings MD, offline sales were reflected in Q1, and pre-ordered online sales will be reflected in Q2.

In Q1, OPM contracted to 13.9% from the album releases of early-stage artists, the absence of large-scale global concerts, artist contract renewal impact, and one-off production cost from INNIT Entertainment.

Content production increased by 53.8% YoY due to the albums of early–stage artists including KickFlip debut album and the reflection of domestic concert production costs. Artist fee increased by 16.4% YoY due to artist contract renewal. Additionally, following the previous quarter, one-time "The Entertainer" production cost of 1.7 BN KRW was reflected in SG&A.

Now, I will present major artists' activities and investment highlights.

JYP Entertainment will strengthen its global leadership position through the expansion of major artists' large-scale world tours and actively develop new global and localized artists to expand our artist lineup. Furthermore, we will continue optimization of the subsidiary's business, while enhancing the fandom experience and monetizing through internalized and integrated platform.

DAY6 is currently on their third world tour, the largest ever with a total of 45 shows in 23 regions. Ongoing KSPO DOME concerts have been completely sold out, and the digital single released on May 7th ranked the highest on the domestic music charts, demonstrating their power as an iconic K-POP band.

TWICE is diversifying their activities with group, unit, and solo projects and will further penetrate global audiences through a new world tour within the year. They also performed as a special guest at COLDPLAY Korea concerts. TWICE will keep solidifying their position as a global top tier artist, performing as a headliner at ‘Lollapalooza Chicago’.

Stray Kids are currently holding a massive world tour with the highest-ever number of audiences of 2.2 million with 55 shows in 34 regions. They plan to release two additional albums within the year and Korea encore show. Also, Stray Kids became the third K-POP group to break 20 million YouTube subscribers last April.

ITZY has diversified their activities with Yeji solo, and they are planning to release a new mini album in June. Active album releases and concerts are planned for this year as well.

NMIXX is successfully holding a total of 20 fan concerts in 12 cities in Latin America, Asia, China region, and Oceania. They are continuously expanding their global mass fandom through activities including their recent performance at the festival in North America.

KickFlip, who debuted this year, is coming back with a 2nd mini album on May 26th. They are performing at global festivals including ‘Lollapalooza Chicago’ and will continue to enhance domestic and global core fandom through diverse activities throughout the year.

NiziU released a global single and Japan mini album. They will further penetrate Japan and global fandom with the largest ever tour in 25 cities in Japan beginning from H2, and fan concerts in China Region in June.

NEXZ who debuted last year are carrying out their activities both in Korea and Japan with the recent 2nd mini album release in Korea. Also, they are planning to release the Japan 2nd EP album on July 16th, with the announcement of additional 2 shows in Tokyo Budokan after one year of their debut. Including these shows, NEXZ is planning to have 18 concerts in Japan from June. They are diversifying their MD with official characters NEXZOO and FOX2Y.

Project C, with their official name ‘CIIU’, recently released a digital single. They are currently releasing digital contents and performing at Chinese local music festivals, and they will officially make a debut in H2. VCHA is planning a comeback with localized content.

In 2026, we are planning to roll out new artists and launch new projects including L2K project and AI artist. JYP Entertainment will actively develop both global and localized early-stage artists, as well as enhance our global business capabilities by penetrating the global market and strengthening partnerships.

Furthermore, we aim to continue investment in enhancing business capabilities to secure mid-to-long-term growth drivers. We will optimize our current business structure by enhancing the multi-label system and creativity capabilities within the company and by diversifying artist IPs.

Blue Garage completed ‘JYP SHOP’ and ‘FANS’ integration and internalization in April. Advanced e-commerce functions have been added to the FANS’ existing community features to enable fans to use community and commerce within one integrated FANS platform. We will utilize integrated data collected within FANS for strategic decisions. Also, we will strive to improve profitability and provide differentiated experiences for fans by introducing paid integrated memberships in H2.

In addition to platform internalization, Blue Garage is in the process of optimizing its MD business. We will improve profitability by significantly reducing offline pop-up stores and strengthening IP licensing collaborations. We will also diversify our MD product categories, including character merchandise such as SKZOO's Tamagotchi collaboration and NEXZ's FOX2Y. Blue Garage will aim to improve profitability through its platform internalization and MD optimization processes in 2025.

Next is the album and performance schedules for 2025. The detailed album and performance schedules disclosed in this slide are based on information currently released through official social accounts and the media, and they are subject to change with future announcements. Especially from Q2, we are expecting the growth mainly from concerts and tour MD as Stray Kids begin their stadium size tours in LATAM, North America, Japan, and Europe.

# Electricity customers get a break on rules

The Ministry of Commerce, Industry and Energy said yesterday that it is responding to customers’ complaints by easing rules on overdue fees and other policies.

Na Do-sung, who oversees electric power policies, said customers could save a total of 18.5 billion won ($18.5 million) a year with the new rules, most of which go into effect today.

For example, the ministry said it would waive the daily charge if power failures last for at least three hours. Before, the waivers were only issued if the power outage was five hours.

Customers who get in trouble with their landlords for nonpayment of rent will no longer have their power cut off automatically at the landlord’s request. The Korea Electric Power Corp. must get the customers’ permission first.

Starting Oct. 1, overdue electric fees will be assessed on a daily basis, rather than the larger monthly basis. Last year, the number of customers who delayed paying late fees exceeded 3.3 million.

by Park Sung-ha
